title: Sedation Emergency – Complete Airway Loss
description: A sedated patient progresses to complete airway obstruction and apnoea requiring escalating airway rescue manoeuvres, bag-mask ventilation, and potential emergency airway management. Emphasises the 'cannot oxygenate' scenario in procedural sedation.
learningObjectives: Recognise complete airway obstruction in a sedated patient
Execute escalating airway rescue: jaw thrust → oral airway → BMV → supraglottic airway → intubation
Apply two-hand bag-mask ventilation technique correctly
Understand OSA and obesity as high-risk factors for airway loss
Determine when to call for anaesthesia/airway expert assistance

states.prompt: Pre-procedure: A 52-year-old male (BMI 42 kg/m²) presents for liver biopsy under moderate sedation. He has known severe OSA (AHI 48), Mallampati III, and a short thick neck. He uses CPAP nightly but did not bring the device. Which risk factor MOST elevates his airway loss risk during sedation?

options.label: Untreated OSA with high AHI (pharyngeal muscle relaxation risk)
Liver disease (altered drug metabolism)
BMI alone is the primary risk (weight on chest wall)
Mallampati III alone is the strongest predictor

explanations: Correct! Untreated high-AHI OSA is the greatest predictor of sedation-induced upper airway obstruction. Opioids and benzodiazepines abolish the arousal response that normally clears obstruction.
Liver disease alters pharmacokinetics but does not directly cause airway obstruction.
Obesity contributes to reduced FRC and pharyngeal crowding, but OSA-specific upper airway collapse is the dominant mechanism here.
Mallampati predicts intubation difficulty more than obstruction risk. OSA with high AHI is the stronger airway loss predictor.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies high-AHI OSA as primary airway loss risk factor

states.prompt: Sedation with propofol 120 mg administered. Biopsy underway. Suddenly: complete cessation of EtCO2 waveform, SpO2 dropping rapidly 98% → 90% → 83%. Chest wall is not moving. You see paradoxical rocking (see-saw motion). What does this indicate?

options.label: Complete upper airway obstruction with obstructed apnoea
Central apnoea – respiratory drive abolished
Capnography probe displacement artefact only
Bronchospasm causing exhalation obstruction

explanations: Correct! Paradoxical (see-saw) chest/abdominal rocking with absent EtCO2 confirms complete upper airway obstruction. The patient is trying to breathe against a closed glottis.
Central apnoea presents with NO respiratory effort (no chest movement at all). See-saw motion indicates active respiratory effort against an obstruction.
EtCO2 disappearance AND SpO2 drop AND paradoxical motion together indicate real airway obstruction, not just a probe issue.
Bronchospasm causes audible wheeze on exhalation and prolonged expiration, not complete absence of EtCO2 with paradoxical motion.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies complete upper airway obstruction

states.prompt: SpO2 is 78% and dropping. Jaw thrust alone does not restore ventilation. What is the CORRECT escalated response in the correct order?

options.label: Insert oropharyngeal airway + two-hand bag-mask ventilation + call for expert airway help
Proceed directly to RSI intubation
Give naloxone 0.4 mg IV and wait for spontaneous breathing
Position the patient prone to open the airway

explanations: Correct! The escalation sequence is: jaw thrust → oral/nasal airway → two-hand BMV → supraglottic airway → intubation. Call for expert help early. Do not delay BMV.
RSI intubation may be needed but is premature at this stage. BMV and supraglottic airways should be attempted first.
Naloxone reverses opioids but not propofol. The SpO2 is critically low — pharmacological reversal is too slow; BMV is the priority.
Prone positioning is not part of acute sedation airway rescue and may further compromise ventilation.

checklistItems.description: Correctly escalates to OPA + bag-mask ventilation and calls for help

states.prompt: Two-person bag-mask ventilation is being attempted for this obese OSA patient but remains difficult. Which technique MOST improves mask seal and ventilation in a difficult BMV patient?

options.label: Two-hand (thenar eminence) E-C clamp technique, second provider squeezes the bag
Apply more force to the mask to improve seal
Increase ventilation rate to 20 breaths/min
Remove the pillow and lay the patient completely flat

explanations: Correct! Two-hand technique with thenar eminence grip provides superior mask seal versus one-hand technique. A second provider squeezes the bag to complete the manoeuvre.
Excessive mask pressure causes air leakage and facial soft-tissue injury. Two-hand seal is superior to increased force.
Hyperventilation is harmful — 10–12 breaths/min with small tidal volumes (6–8 mL/kg IBW) avoids gastric inflation and auto-PEEP.
Ramped or elevated head position (reverse Trendelenburg) IMPROVES BMV in obese patients. Flat positioning worsens airway obstruction.

checklistItems.description: Identifies two-hand thenar eminence technique for difficult BMV

states.prompt: SpO2 recovers to 95% with BMV. The anaesthesiologist arrives. Propofol is stopped. The patient begins making spontaneous effort. What is the safest monitoring plan for the recovery period?

options.label: Continuous capnography + SpO2 monitoring until full MOASS recovery; consider CPAP for OSA
Transfer to PACU on room air when SpO2 ≥ 94%
Supplemental O2 alone is sufficient for recovery monitoring
Discharge home once the patient is awake and communicating

explanations: Correct! OSA patients require continuous capnography and SpO2 throughout recovery. CPAP should be applied post-procedure. Supplemental O2 masks hypoventilation in OSA patients.
OSA patients on supplemental O2 can maintain SpO2 ≥ 94% even during significant hypoventilation. EtCO2 monitoring is essential.
O2 alone is insufficient — it masks hypoventilation in OSA. Continuous capnography is standard of care.
Post-sedation airway emergency requires extended monitoring (PACU or equivalent); same-day discharge is inappropriate.

checklistItems.description: Plans continuous capnography and CPAP for OSA recovery

states.prompt:
Scenario complete. Review your performance summary below.

📚 Key Teaching Points:
• OSA + obesity = high-risk airway; have escalation plan before starting sedation
• Paradoxical (see-saw) motion = obstructive apnoea, not central apnoea
• Escalation: jaw thrust → OPA → 2-hand BMV → SGA → intubation
• OSA recovery: continuous capnography, CPAP; supplemental O2 alone masks hypoventilation
